deck: Boson Challenge Lab 1 - Dual-Stack IPv4 + IPv6.apkg

cards.front: On a Layer 3 switch SVI, which IPv6 command makes the interface act as a SLAAC client (listens for RAs)?
cards.back:
ipv6 address autoconfig
(NOT ipv6 address X::/64 eui-64 — that's for manual prefix + auto host bits, different command path.)

cards.front: Cisco IOS command to assign an IPv6 address with manual prefix but auto-generated EUI-64 host bits?
cards.back:
ipv6 address <prefix>::/64 eui-64
(Router uses its MAC to build the lower 64 bits.)

cards.front: Cisco IOS command to enable IPv6 routing globally on a router?
cards.back:
ipv6 unicast-routing (global config mode)
Required on routers to forward IPv6 packets and send RAs. NOT needed on a switch acting only as a SLAAC client.

cards.front: NetSim PC command to assign an IPv4 address and subnet mask?
cards.back:
ipconfig /ip <address> <mask>
Example: ipconfig /ip 192.168.100.8 255.255.255.240

cards.front: NetSim PC command to set the default gateway?
cards.back:
ipconfig /dg <gateway>
Example: ipconfig /dg 192.168.100.1

cards.front: NetSim PC command to trigger IPv6 SLAAC?
cards.back:
ipconfig /ipv6 autoconfig
(Note: /ipv6 flag, not ipv6 address Cisco-style.)
cards.tags: lab1 commands host netsim ipv6 slaac

cards.front: Command to configure a default gateway on a Layer 2 switch (so it can be managed remotely)?
cards.back:
ip default-gateway <address> (global config mode)
Used because an L2-only switch can't route — only forwards mgmt traffic to the gateway.

cards.front: Verify-command: brief summary of all IPv6 interfaces?
cards.back: show ipv6 interface brief

cards.front: Verify-command: detailed IPv6 info for one specific SVI?
cards.back:
show ipv6 interface vlan <N>
Look for: "Stateless address autoconfig enabled" + global address + link-local.

cards.front: Two ways to save the running configuration to startup config?
cards.back:
wr (short form)
copy running-config startup-config (long form)
If you reload without saving, your config evaporates.

cards.front: What does SLAAC stand for?
cards.back:
Stateless Address Autoconfiguration
A device builds its own IPv6 address using the prefix it learns from a Router Advertisement (RA) + an interface ID it generates locally (usually EUI-64).

cards.front: EUI-64 derivation: starting from MAC 000C.4260.8261, what are the 4 steps to build the interface ID?
cards.back:
1. Split MAC into OUI (first 6 hex) + serial (last 6 hex): 000C42 + 608261
2. Insert FFFE between OUI and serial: 000C42FFFE608261
3. Regroup as IPv6 hextets: 000C:42FF:FE60:8261
4. Flip the 7th bit (U/L bit) of the first octet: 00 → 02, giving 020C:42FF:FE60:8261

cards.front: In modified EUI-64, where is the U/L (universal/local) bit and what does flipping it do?
cards.back:
Bit 7 of the first octet (counting from left, 1-indexed).
Flip: 0 → 1 if globally unique, 1 → 0 if locally administered.
Effect on hex: second hex char changes (e.g. 00→02, 02→00).

cards.front: IPv6 link-local address prefix?
cards.back:
FE80::/10
Typically begins with FE8, FE9, FEA, or FEB.
Every IPv6 interface MUST have a link-local — required by spec.

cards.front: IPv6 global unicast addresses typically begin with which hex digit?
cards.back:
2 or 3
Documentation/example range: 2001:DB8::/32 (RFC 3849).

cards.front: IPv6 multicast prefix?
cards.back:
FF00::/8 (all multicast starts with hex FF)
Common groups seen on interfaces: FF02::1 (all nodes), FF02::2 (all routers), FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx (solicited-node).

cards.front: IPv6 unique local address (ULA) prefixes?
cards.back:
FC00::/7 — addresses begin with FC or FD
(Analog to IPv4 RFC 1918 private space.)

cards.front: For SLAAC to give a switch SVI a global IPv6 address, what must be true on the upstream router?
cards.back:
Router must:
1. Have ipv6 unicast-routing enabled globally
2. Have an IPv6 address on the LAN-facing interface with the prefix you want clients to use
3. Be sending Router Advertisements (RAs) — default behavior once IPv6 is enabled

cards.front: Does IPv6 have a broadcast address type?
cards.back:
No. IPv6 eliminated broadcast.
Broadcast functions are split across:
• Multicast — send to multiple specific devices
• Anycast — send to the closest device in a group

cards.front: How many usable host addresses in a /30 subnet?
cards.back:
2 usable (4 total - 2 reserved for network/broadcast)
Mask = 255.255.255.252. Used for point-to-point WAN links.

cards.front: How many usable host addresses in a /28 subnet?
cards.back:
14 usable (16 total - 2 reserved)
Mask = 255.255.255.240.

cards.front: What is a /32 IPv4 subnet used for?
cards.back:
A single host route — the network IS the broadcast IS the address.
Mask = 255.255.255.255. Common use: loopback interfaces on routers.

cards.front: Subnet mask 255.255.255.252 in CIDR notation?
cards.back:
/30
30 bits set to 1, 2 bits set to 0 in the host portion.

cards.front: Subnet mask 255.255.255.240 in CIDR notation?
cards.back:
/28
Last octet: 11110000 = 240. Host portion = 4 bits = 2^4 = 16 addresses.

cards.front: Three RFC 1918 private IPv4 ranges?
TROUBLESHOOTING: You typed ipv6 address 2001:DB8::/64 eui-64 on an SVI and got % Invalid input detected at '^' marker. Fix?
cards.back:
Use ipv6 address autoconfig instead.
SVI's IPv6 parser doesn't accept the eui-64 keyword in NetSim. autoconfig = full SLAAC (prefix from RA + auto host bits). The eui-64 keyword is for when YOU supply the prefix manually.

cards.front: TROUBLESHOOTING: SLAAC enabled on a switch SVI but no global IPv6 address appears in show ipv6 interface vlan 1. What to check?
cards.back:
1. Upstream router has ipv6 unicast-routing enabled
2. Router's LAN interface has a global IPv6 address in the desired prefix
3. Router and switch are L2-adjacent (no broken VLAN/trunk)
4. SVI is no shutdown and line protocol up

cards.front: BOSON GRADER: Yellow highlight on LEFT column only ("Your Running-Config"), expected column blank. What does this mean and how do you fix?
cards.back:
You have EXTRA lines the grader doesn't want.
Fix: Remove those lines from your config. E.g., no ipv6 unicast-routing if it shouldn't be on this device.

cards.front: BOSON GRADER: Yellow highlight on RIGHT column only ("Expected Running-Config"), your column blank. What does this mean and how do you fix?
cards.back:
You are MISSING required lines.
Fix: Add the missing commands to your config and save (wr).

cards.front: After configuring IPv6 with ipv6 address on a router interface that already has an IPv4 address, does the IPv4 address get erased?
cards.back:
No. IPv4 and IPv6 coexist on the same interface — this is called dual-stack.
Confirm with show running-config: both ip address and ipv6 address lines appear under the same interface.

cards.front: After no shutdown, Serial 0/0 transitions up → then immediately back down. Why?
cards.back:
The other end of the serial link is not configured / not enabled.
For a line protocol to stay up, both ends need to be configured on the same subnet with matching encapsulation (HDLC by default on Cisco).

cards.front: Does Boson grader read your running-config or your startup-config when grading?
cards.back:
Running-config.
BUT: if the lab session reloads (or you simulate a reload), unsaved running-config is lost. Always wr before grading to be safe.

cards.front: RED HERRING: "My switch rejects ipv6 address X::/64 eui-64 on SVI — is it because the SDM template doesn't allocate IPv6 TCAM?"
cards.back:
No, that's wrong for NetSim.
Real Catalyst gear sometimes needs sdm prefer dual-ipv4-and-ipv6 + reload, but Boson NetSim uses a simpler image without SDM partitioning.
Actual cause: wrong keyword. Use autoconfig for SLAAC on an SVI.

cards.front: Boson CCNA Challenge Lab 1: how many devices in the topology?
cards.back:
10 devices total
• 2 routers (Router1, Router2)
• 2 distribution switches (DSW1, DSW2)
• 3 access switches (ASW1, ASW2, ASW3)
• 3 hosts (Host1, Host2, Host3)

cards.front: Lab 1: which IPv4 range was used for the point-to-point WAN link between Router1 and Router2, and what /XX subnet was carved from it?
cards.back:
Range: 203.0.113.0/24 (global unicast example)
Subnet used: 203.0.113.0/30 (2 usable: .1 and .2)
R1 S0/0 = 203.0.113.1, R2 S0/0 = 203.0.113.2

cards.front: Lab 1: which IPv4 range was used for the LAN, and what /XX was carved?
cards.back:
Range: 192.168.100.0/24 (RFC 1918 private)
Subnet: 192.168.100.0/28 — 16 addresses (.0-.15), 14 usable.

cards.front: Lab 1: how were loopback interfaces on R1 and R2 subnetted?
cards.back:
Two contiguous /32 subnets from 192.168.100.0/24:
R1 Loopback0 = 192.168.100.16/32
R2 Loopback0 = 192.168.100.17/32
(R1 lower than R2 per instructions.)

cards.front: Lab 1: what IPv6 /64 prefix was used across the network?
cards.back:
2001:DB8:3445:ABDE::/64
(2001:DB8::/32 is the documentation prefix per RFC 3849.)

cards.front: Lab 1: what IPv6 address was manually configured on Router1 FA1/0 vs Router2 FA1/0?
cards.back:
R1 FA1/0 = 2001:DB8:3445:ABDE::1/64
R2 FA1/0 = 2001:DB8:3445:ABDE::2/64
(R1 lower than R2 per instructions.)

cards.front: Lab 1: default gateway used by all switches and all hosts?
cards.back:
192.168.100.1 = Router1's FastEthernet1/0 address
Configured on L2 switches via ip default-gateway 192.168.100.1 (mgmt only).
Configured on hosts via ipconfig /dg 192.168.100.1 (real routing target).

cards.front: VISUAL: complete the EUI-64 derivation. MAC 000C.84??.???? on DSW1 → final SLAAC global address observed was 2001:DB8:3445:ABDE:==BLANK==:84FF:FE99:1946
cards.back:
20C
Working: OUI 000C84, serial 991946, insert FFFE → 000C:84FF:FE99:1946, flip U/L bit on first octet 00→02 → 020C:84FF:FE99:1946, drop leading zero → 20C:84FF:FE99:1946.

cards.front:
VISUAL: ASCII topology of Boson Lab 1 — fill in missing connections.
HostA  HostB    HostC
  \   /         |
   Router1 -- DSW1 -- ASW1
      |               \
   [WAN /30]            Host1
      |
   Router2 -- DSW2 -- ASW2 -- Host2
                  \
                   ASW3 -- Host3
(Original lab — pre-IPv6, IPv4-only. What did you do next?)
cards.back:
Added IPv6 in two layers:
1. Manual on R1+R2 FA1/0 (the prefix source for SLAAC)
2. SLAAC on DSW1, DSW2 vlan 1 + Host1, Host2, Host3 via autoconfig
ASW1-3 stayed L2-only — no IPv6 needed.

cards.front:
VISUAL: complete the SLAAC flow.
Router1 FA1/0 (2001:DB8:3445:ABDE::1/64)
   |
   v
[ ?? ] sent every 200 seconds
   |
   v
DSW1 vlan 1 hears prefix → builds EUI-64 host bits → installs global address
cards.back:
Router Advertisement (RA)
Sent on all IPv6-enabled router interfaces by default once ipv6 unicast-routing is on.
Carries: prefix, prefix lifetime, default-router preference, hop limit.

cards.front: QUESTION (Romeo, mid-lab): "Am I missing something?" after grading showed a red X on DSW1. BTW — what's the first thing to check?
cards.back:
Diff direction. Open the failing device tab in the grader.
Left column = your config. Right column = expected.
Yellow on LEFT only = you have extras (remove them).
Yellow on RIGHT only = you're missing lines (add them).
Don't assume "missed" means "missing" — it could mean "extra."

cards.front: QUESTION (Romeo, mid-lab): "What's next after I run ipv6 address autoconfig on both DSW1 and DSW2 vlan 1?" BTW — what sequence completes Task 2?
cards.back:
1. Verify DSW1 + DSW2 picked up SLAAC: show ipv6 interface vlan 1 — global addr present?
2. Hosts: ipconfig /ipv6 autoconfig on Host1, Host2, Host3
3. Verify hosts: plain ipconfig — see IPv6 Address: 2001:DB8:3445:ABDE:...
4. wr on every router and switch
5. Grade

cards.front: QUESTION: Why does ipv6 address X::/64 eui-64 fail on a switch SVI when it works fine on a router FastEthernet interface? BTW what's the actual rule?
cards.back:
SVI's IPv6 parser in NetSim only accepts ipv6 address autoconfig (full SLAAC) for auto-generated addressing on SVIs.
The eui-64 keyword path requires you to supply the prefix — it's a different command structure intended for routed interfaces.
Rule of thumb: SVI + want SLAAC = autoconfig. Routed interface + manual prefix + auto host = eui-64.

cards.front: QUESTION: Does an L3 switch acting as a SLAAC client need ipv6 unicast-routing enabled globally? BTW — why or why not?
cards.back:
No.
SLAAC client only listens for RAs and configures its own SVI — it's not forwarding IPv6 packets between subnets.
Only devices that route IPv6 (routers, or L3 switches doing inter-VLAN IPv6 routing) need the global enable.
Boson grader flags ipv6 unicast-routing on a SLAAC-only switch as an EXTRA to remove.
